California
Fair Political
Practices Comrnission
September 14, 199o
Peter A. Bagatelos
Bagatelos & Fadem
The International Building
601 california Street, Suite 1801
San Francisco, cA 9 4108
Re:
Your Request for Informal Assistance
our File No. I-9 o-4 57
Dear llr. Bagatelos:
This is in response to your Letter of July 2, 1990, request-
ing advice regarding contribution lirnits under the Political
Reiorn Act (the na.!n1.1 specificallYr You have requested
confirmation of certain telephone advice given by rne in a
conversation with you on July 2, 1990. Since you have not
provided specific facts required for formal advice, this response
to your Letter will be in the nature of informal- assistance,
pur'suant to Regulation 18329(c) (copy enclosed).2
OUESTI ONS
L. what contribution limits apply $rhen two general partners'
each of whon has a 50 percent interest in a partnership (the
trlarger partnershiprt) which controls 10-15 other partnerships (the
ttsmai.ler-partnershlpsrr), each make an individual contri-bution to
one candj.date, and none of the partnerships contributes?
2. what contribution limits apply when one or more of the
partnerships contribute to a single candidate?
3. What contribution linits 
apply when one or both of the
individual partners and one or more of the partnerships make
contributions to one candidate?
1 Go.r"rr,."nt code sections 81ooo-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government code unless otherwise indicated. 
commission
regulations appear at 2 california Code of Regulations Section
18ooo, et seq. Al-1 references to regulations are to Titl"e 2,
Division 6 of the california code of Regul,ations.
2 rnformal assistance does not
irnmunity provided bY an oPinion
83114; Regulation 18329 (c) (3).)
provide the requestor with the
or forrnal written advice. (Section
428 J Street, Suite 8OO a P.O. Box 8O7 a Sacramento CA 958O4.O8O7 . (916)322-s66O


Fi■e No. ■-90-457
Page 2
1. If the partners
by the partnerships, then
controlled the decisions
individually nay give up
cal year.
CONCI,USIONS
nake joint decisions as to contributions
neither partner has directed or
to contribute and eactr partner
to 91,ooo to a singLe candidate in a fis-
2. Business entities with the same controlling owner are
considered one person for purposes of cuuulatinq contributions.
Hence the aggregated contribution from all the partnerships
controlled by the two partners herein cannot exceed a total of
$1, 000.
3. If neither partner controls the decisions of any of the
partnerships to contribute, then each partner may contribute up to
$1,ooo. The partnership contributions may not aggregate more than
$1,ooo, whether or not either or both of the partners makes an
individual contribution.
FACTS
You represent a partnership owned by two 50 Percent partners.
This partnership, in turn, controls 10-15 other partnerships.
Partnership decisions regarding political contributions are
made jointly by the partners. Neither partner controls partner-
ship decisions or the other partner.
on July 2, ]-ggo, you received telephone advice from me
regarding cumulation of contributions. 
you have written the com-
rniision io obtain confirmation of the telephone advice in light of
your reading of the olson Advice Letter, No. A-90-302 (copy
enclosed), *trictr you perceive to be inconsistent with the advice
tendered to you on July 2, L99o.
DISCUSSION
The Act, as amended by Proposition 73, provides that
contributions frou persons to candidates for elective office nust
conpty with fiscal year contribution linits 
as set forth in sec-
tions 85301 , 85302 and 85303. The purpose of the contribution
linitation 
provisions of Proposition 73 was to place a reasonable
ceiling on the anount that could be contributed by one donor to a
candidate. See Recht Advice Letter, No. I-89-571 (copy enclosed)
citing Argument in Favor of Proposition 73, California Ba1lot
Panphlet, June 7, 1988, Primary Election, p.34.
Under the Act there are tlto lines of authority which require
that contributions by nore than one person be cumulated and
treated as single contributors. 
The standards for circumstances
requiring such cumulation were first set forth in 1976 in two


File No. t-90-457
Page 3
opinions, fn Re Kahn, 2 FPPC Ops. 151 and In Re Lumsdon, 2 FPPC
ops. 140 (copies enclosed) . The Kahn opinion concludes that a
cumulation of contributions j,s required (1) lrhere contributions
were made by both a parent corporation and its wholly oerned
subsidiaries, and (2) where the parent corporation made no
contribution, but the subsidiaries did contrj.bute. Cumulation $ras
not required where surrounding circumstances made it clear that
the corporations made their contributions conpletely independently
of each other.
In the Lunsden opinion, the commission found that contribu-
tions of a closely held corporation and the najority shareholder
thereof required cumulation. In both Kahn and Lunsden, the
pivotal question sras one of control of the contributions of one
entity by another entity or person.
More recent advice letters have been consistent with Kahn and
Lumsden. The Recht Advice Letter, supra, concerns, in part, the
cumulation of contributions by a partnership whose contributions
are controlled by a steering cornmittee rnade up of five of 50
partners. Among the questions asked in the Recht letter was
whether the contributions of the partnership nust be cunulated
with those of the five controlling partners. The advice given r,ras
that since no sinqle nember of the steering connittee actually
directed and controlled the partnership, cumulation of contribu-
tj.ons made by the partnership and the individual members of the
steeringT cornmittee was not required.
Further advice in the Recht letter involved a general
partnership consistj.ng of two corporate partners. The contribu-
tions of the partnership were controlled by two individuals, one
of whom was president of one of the corporate partners and the
other, the chairman and chief executive officer of the sane
corporation. In that factual situation, it was determined that
contributions of the partnership \^rould not be curnulated with those
of the controlling individuals unless one of the individuals, in
fact, directed and controlled the contributions of the partnership
or the corporation.
In May, 1990, the Commission rendered advice in the Olson
Advice Letter, supra, to which you have made reference in your
letter. 
The Olson letter, as you correctly noted, concluded that
it was permissible for an official 
to accept contributions fron
two general partners of a business entity even though the entity
itself had previously made a contribution of $1,000 to the
official's 
carnpaign. The reason for the decision rendered in
qIEgI rras that neither of the two general partners of the business
entity who wished to make individual contributions had directed or
controlled the decision to contribute funds fron the partnership
to the official; 
the third general partner who did direct and
control the entity's contribution would be required to cunulate
his or her contributj,ons with that of the entity.


File No. I-9o-457
Page 4
Requlation 18531,.53 seeks to clarify the cumulation issue
eihere there is a question regarding cumulations of contributions
frorn affiliated entities, 
Regulation 18531.5 sets forth the fol-
losring:
(a) If the same person or a maioritv of the
same persons in fact directs and control.s the deci-
sions of two or more entities to make contributions
or expenditures to support or oppose a candidate or
candidates for elective office, those affiliated
entities shaII be consi.dered one person. one
political 
cornrnittee, or one broad based political
comrnittee for purooses of the contribution limita-
tions in covernment code Sections 85301, 85302.
85303 and 85305.
(b) Business entities in a parent-subs idiarv
relationship and business entities with the same
controllinq (more than so-percent) orrner shall be
considered one person for purposes of the contribu-
tion limitations in Government code Sections 85301.
85302. 85303 and 85305, unless the business enti-
ties act completelv independentlv in their deci-
sions to rnake contributions and expenditures to
suDDort or oDDose candidates for elective office.
rnore than 50 percent of another business entitv.
***
My advice to you in our conversation of JuIy 2, 1990, was
consistent with both previous advice given in FPPC advice Letters
and also $rith ReguLation 18531.5. The first question you posited
concerned two equal partners who 'iointlv decided that their
partnership entity should contribute $1,Ooo to a campaign. since
neither partner directed or controlled the decision to contribute,
the entity's contribution would not be cumulated with those of the
individual partners, Per Kahn, EUtrIEa, Lumsden, E!P!!! r Begh!,
.g.llpr!! r and Olson, supra . Thus each partner would be permitted to
contribute $1,000.
The second query in your letter concerned cumuLation of
contributions there one or more of the partnerships controlled by
your clients made contributions to the sarne candidate. Regulation
3 negulation 18531.5 is in the rulernaking process. Atthough not
curre;tIy effective, the regulatj.on expresses the Conmission's
policy concerning curnulation of contributions.


File No. I-9o-457
Page 5
18531.5 requires cumulation of contributions when business entj,-
ties are in a parent-subs idiary relationship or rrhen business
entities have the same controlling owner. Since the smaller
partnerships are under the control of the larger partnership, the
partnerships would be considered to be one person under Regulation
18531.5(b), and the maximum contribution from the conbined
partnerships would be $1,000. The amounts given by the different
partnerships would not matter, so long as the sum of all the
partnership contributions to one candidate for one fiscal year did
not aggregate to more than $L,000.
Your final question postulates one or both of the individual,
partners and one or nore of the partnerships roaking contributions
to a single candidate. Again, if the decision regarding the
contribution of any of the partnerships is nade jointly, 
then
neither partner has controlled the decision and each partner may
individual-ty contribute $1,ooo. ff the joint decision were to
make a maximum contribution fron any of the partnerships then that
maximum could be made by any of the partnerships, but no further
contribution from any of the partnerships would be pernitted to
the recipient candidate during that fiscaL year.
If one of the partners does control the decision to
contribute partnership funds, then any individual contribution by
that partner would have to be agqregated with the funds
contributed by the partnership. The other partner could make an
individual contribution of up to $1,000 without any cumulation of
partnership contributions.
The pivotal issue, in both the advice letters cited and also
in Regulation 18531.5, is one of control. 
where a business entity
acts -onpletely independently of any parent or affiliated 
entity
in its decision to nake a contribution to a candidate, there is no
requirement to curnulate contributions.
The olson letter, which you believe is inconsistent with the
advice tendered to you in our telephone conversation, is actuafly
quite consistent with $/hat you were told, but olson deals only
witn a matter analogous to the substance of your first question
and does not touch on curnulation of contributions between enti-
ties. 
The conclusion in olson is that t$/o general partners who
did not direct and control the contribution of a partnership did
not have to aggregate their contributions with those of the
partnership. This is precisely the advice given to you in our
conversation of July 2, ]-990.


I-90-457
File
Page
SH:SLB:p■h
Enc■OSures
No.
6
If you require further info..:ation from us regarding cumu■
a―
tion of contr■butions, please do not hes■
tate to contact me at
(9■6)322-590■
.
sincerely′
Scott Hallabrin
Acting ceneral
Susan L. Bobrow
Counsel, Legal Division
By:
